days.title: Много и мало: a lot and a little

vocab.exampleEn: We have a lot of water.
I drink a lot of water.
There is little bread at home.
There is a lot of sugar in the tea.

grammar.title: Много / мало + genitive (uncountable nouns)
grammar.explanation:
After **много** (a lot) and **мало** (little/few) you use the **genitive case**, not the nominative. With uncountable nouns (water, bread, milk) you use the **genitive singular**.

For most masculine and neuter nouns, the genitive singular ends in **-а / -я**: хлеб → хлеб**а**, молоко → молок**а**, сахар → сахар**а**.

For feminine nouns ending in **-а**, change to **-ы**: вода → вод**ы**. For feminine nouns in **-ь**, change to **-и**: соль → сол**и**.

examples.english: We have a lot of bread.
There is little water at home.
There is a lot of milk in the cup.

reading.title: Дома
reading.text: — Дома есть хлеб? — Нет, хлеба мало. А воды много.
reading.translation: — Is there bread at home? — No, there is little bread. But there is a lot of water.

days.speakingPrompt: Look in your kitchen. Say one thing you have a lot of and one thing you have little of, using много and мало plus the genitive (e.g. У меня много воды, но мало хлеба).

days.title: Немного: a little bit

vocab.exampleEn: In the morning I drink coffee.
There is a little juice here.
There is a little butter on the bread.

grammar.title: Немного + genitive (a small amount)
grammar.explanation:
**Немного** means "a little / a bit" and, like много and мало, it takes the **genitive case**. It is used for a small but positive amount.

The genitive singular endings are the same as yesterday: masculine/neuter → **-а / -я** (чай → ча**я**, масло → масл**а**), feminine **-а** → **-ы**.

Note: **кофе** does not change — it is an indeclinable masculine noun: *немного кофе*.

examples.english: There is a little juice here.
In the morning I drink a little coffee.

reading.title: Завтрак
reading.text: — Что ты хочешь? — Немного чая и немного сыра.
reading.translation: — What do you want? — A little tea and a little cheese.

days.speakingPrompt: Imagine ordering a light breakfast. Ask for two items using немного plus the genitive (e.g. Я хочу немного чая и немного масла).

days.title: Containers: бутылка, чашка, стакан

vocab.exampleEn: This is a bottle of water.
I want a glass of juice.
This is a carton of milk.
A plate of soup is on the table.
I want a plate of soup.

grammar.title: Container + genitive (a bottle of...)
grammar.explanation:
To say "a cup **of** tea" or "a bottle **of** water", Russian puts the **second noun in the genitive case** — there is no separate word for "of".

*чашка ча**я*** = a cup of tea; *бутылка вод**ы*** = a bottle of water; *стакан сок**а*** = a glass of juice.

The genitive singular endings are the same ones you already know: masculine/neuter **-а / -я**, feminine **-а → -ы**.

examples.english: This is a cup of tea.
I want a glass of juice.
A carton of milk is on the table.

reading.title: В кафе
reading.text: — Что вы хотите? — Чашку чая и стакан воды, пожалуйста.
reading.translation: — What do you want? — A cup of tea and a glass of water, please.

days.speakingPrompt: Order three things in a café using container + genitive (e.g. Чашку кофе, бутылку воды и тарелку супа, пожалуйста).

days.title: Weights: килограмм, грамм, литр

vocab.exampleEn: I want a kilogram of fish.

grammar.title: Measures of weight + genitive
grammar.explanation:
Units of weight and volume behave like containers: the thing measured goes in the **genitive case**.

*килограмм сыр**а*** = a kilo of cheese; *литр молок**а*** = a litre of milk; *кусок хлеб**а*** = a piece of bread.

With feminine nouns: *килограмм рыб**ы*** = a kilo of fish. The measure word itself (килограмм, литр) stays in its base form when you say just "a" of something.

examples.english: I want a kilogram of fish.

reading.title: В магазине
reading.text: — Что вы хотите? — Килограмм рыбы и литр молока, пожалуйста.
reading.translation: — What do you want? — A kilogram of fish and a litre of milk, please.

days.speakingPrompt: You are at a market. Ask for three items using measures plus the genitive (e.g. Килограмм мяса, литр молока и кусок сыра, пожалуйста).

days.title: Genitive plural basics: 5+ and many

vocab.exampleEn: There are many apples on the table.

grammar.title: Genitive plural after много and 5–20
grammar.explanation:
With **countable** things, **много** ("many") and the numbers **5 and up** (5, 6, 7... up to 20) take the **genitive plural**.

A common pattern for masculine nouns is to add **-ов**: банан → банан**ов**, помидор → помидор**ов**.

Many neuter and feminine nouns have a **zero ending** (just drop the final vowel): яблоко → **яблок**, яйцо → **яиц** (irregular), штука → **штук**.

So: *пять бананов*, *много яблок*, *десять яиц*.

reading.title: На рынке
reading.text: — Сколько яблок вы хотите? — Шесть яблок и пять бананов, пожалуйста.
reading.translation: — How many apples do you want? — Six apples and five bananas, please.

days.speakingPrompt: Ask how many of something there are, then order 5 or more of three fruits/vegetables using the genitive plural (e.g. Пять бананов, шесть помидоров и много яблок).

days.title: Numbers 2–4 vs 5+: choosing the form

grammar.title: 2, 3, 4 + genitive singular; 5+ + genitive plural
grammar.explanation:
Russian numbers split into two groups:

**2, 3, 4** take the **genitive singular**: *два банан**а***, *три яблок**а***, *четыре чашк**и***.

**5 and above** take the **genitive plural**: *пять банан**ов***, *пять яблок*, *пять чашек*.

The number **2** has two forms: **два** with masculine/neuter nouns, **две** with feminine nouns (две бутылк**и**, две чашк**и**). The number 1 uses the nominative: *один банан*.

reading.title: Покупки
reading.text: — Два огурца, три помидора и пять яблок, пожалуйста. — И ещё бутылку воды.
reading.translation: — Two cucumbers, three tomatoes and five apples, please. — And also a bottle of water.

days.speakingPrompt: Make a shopping list out loud with five items: use 2, 3 or 4 with two of them and 5+ with the others, choosing the right genitive form (e.g. Два банана, три яблока и пять помидоров).

days.title: Review: Quantities

grammar.title: Recap: quantities and the genitive
grammar.explanation:
This week everything pointed to the **genitive case**:

- **много / мало / немного / сколько** + genitive: singular for uncountables (много вод**ы**), plural for countables (много яблок).
- **Containers and measures** + genitive: чашка ча**я**, литр молок**а**, килограмм рыб**ы**.
- **Numbers**: **2, 3, 4** + genitive singular (три яблок**а**); **5 and up** + genitive plural (пять яблок). Remember **два** (m/n) vs **две** (f).

examples.russian: У нас мало хлеба, но много воды.
examples.english: We have little bread, but a lot of water.

examples.russian: Чашку чая и кусок сыра, пожалуйста.
examples.english: A cup of tea and a piece of cheese, please.

reading.title: В магазине
reading.text: — Что вы хотите? — Два огурца, килограмм рыбы и бутылку воды. — А молоко? — Немного молока, пожалуйста.
reading.translation: — What do you want? — Two cucumbers, a kilogram of fish and a bottle of water. — And milk? — A little milk, please.

days.speakingPrompt: Role-play a full shopping trip. Buy at least six things using a mix of много/мало/немного, containers and measures, and numbers (both 2–4 and 5+) with the correct genitive forms.
